loopRuns.title: Skill Installer
loopRuns.summary: Consolidated fresh signals, added a pre-install security check tactic, a reusable agent prompt for advisory scans, and removed update-engine/refresh noise.
loopRuns.whatChanged:
- Removed update-engine / latest-refresh / recent-log noise and duplicated blocks.
- Consolidated fresh signals into a single summary.
- Added one new tactic: a pre-install security check that queries GitHub advisories and inspects dependencies.
- Added a reusable agent prompt for automated pre-install advisory scans.
- Minor copy cleanup for terseness.
messages: Agent is rewriting the skill body from the fetched source deltas.
v8 is live with body edits.

items.title: Sign in
Search syntax tips
Sign up
Top 10 web hacking techniques of 2025
items.summary: Welcome to the Top 10 Web Hacking Techniques of 2025, the 19th edition of our annual community-powered effort to identify the most innovative must-read web security research published in the last year

items.title: Top 10 web hacking techniques of 2025: call for nominations
items.summary: Update: nominations are now closed, and voting is live! Cast your vote here Over the last year, security researchers have shared a huge amount of work with the community through blog posts, presentati

items.title: The Fragile Lock: Novel Bypasses For SAML Authentication
items.summary: TLDR This post shows how to achieve a full authentication bypass in the Ruby and PHP SAML ecosystem by exploiting several parser-level inconsistencies: including attribute pollution, namespace confusi

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.summary: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.summary: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.summary: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

diffLines.value: Helps install skills. By default these are from https://github.com/openai/skills/tree/main/skills/.curated, but users can also provide other locations. Experimental skills live in https://github.com/openai/skills/tree/main/skills/.experimental and can be installed the same way.
Use the helper scripts based on the task:

- List skills when the user asks what is available, or if the user uses this skill without specifying what to do. Default listing is `.curated`, but you can pass `--path skills/.experimental` when they ask about experimental skills.
- Install from the curated list when the user provides a skill name.
- Install from another repo when the user provides a GitHub repo/path (including private repos).
Install skills with the helper scripts.

When listing skills, output approximately as follows, depending on the context of the user's request. If they ask about experimental skills, list from `.experimental` instead of `.curated` and label the source accordingly:
Which ones would you like installed?
After installing a skill, tell the user: "Restart Codex to pick up new skills."
All of these scripts use network, so when running in the sandbox, request escalation when running them.
- Defaults to direct download for public GitHub repos.
- If download fails with auth/permission errors, falls back to git sparse checkout.
- Aborts if the destination skill directory already exists.
- Multiple `--path` values install multiple skills in one run, each named from the path basename unless `--name` is supplied.
- Curated listing is fetched from `https://github.com/openai/skills/tree/main/skills/.curated` via the GitHub API. If it is unavailable, explain the error and exit.
- Private GitHub repos can be accessed via existing git credentials or optional `GITHUB_TOKEN`/`GH_TOKEN` for download.
- Git fallback tries HTTPS first, then SSH.

- The skills at https://github.com/openai/skills/tree/main/skills/.system are preinstalled, so no need to help users install those. If they ask, just explain this. If they insist, you can download and overwrite.
The biggest delta is Sign up. Fold the concrete changes into the operating notes, then discard the fluff.
- GitHub advisories: multiple OpenClaw advisories affecting webhooks, gateway auth and privilege escalation; a path-to-regexp regular-expression DoS advisory.
- PortSwigger research: recent techniques (Top 10 Web Hacking Techniques 2025), SAML bypasses and HTTP desync/request-smuggling analysis.
- KrebsOnSecurity: active botnets and disruptive DDoS (Kimwolf), phishing-as-a-service that proxies real login pages, and guidance about AI assistants shifting security priorities.
- [OpenClaw: Telegram Webhook Missing Guess Rate Limiting Enables Brute-Force Guessing of Weak Webhook Secret](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-vcx4-4qxg-mfp4) · Github advisories
- [OpenClaw: BlueBubbles Group Reactions Bypass requireMention and Still Enqueue Agent-Visible System Events](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-mw7w-g3mg-xqm7) · Github advisories
- [OpenClaw: Matrix Verification Notices Bypass Matrix DM Policy and Reply to Unpaired DM Peers](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-9wqx-g2cw-vc7r) · Github advisories
Concrete changes folded into operating notes:
- Before installing any skill from an external repo, run a quick advisory + dependency check and require explicit user confirmation if any issue is found. Practical checks:
- Query GitHub advisories for the repo or package names referenced by the skill (look for advisories like OpenClaw or path-to-regexp).
- Inspect package manifests (package.json, gemspec, pyproject) for known vulnerable dependencies.
- If the repo contains webhooks or gateway code, flag for manual review (rate-limiting, auth scoping).
- If high/critical advisories are present, abort installation and report the findings.
- Add one new tactic based on Sign up.
- Rewrite one stale section with today’s source language.
- Turn the top signal into a reusable agent prompt.
2) Reusable agent prompt (for automated pre-install checks)
Use this prompt when running an agent that evaluates a candidate repo before install:

"Check advisories and dependencies for <repo_url>. List any GitHub advisories affecting files or deps (highlight CVE/GHSA IDs), flag high/critical issues (stop install), and summarize recommended actions. If no issues, return OK to install."

- Removed duplicated and noisy refresh/update-engine blocks. The product tracks update-engine/observability separately; keep the skill content focused on user-facing behavior and pre-install security checks.
- [OpenClaw: Telegram Webhook Missing Guess Rate Limiting Enables Brute-Force Guessing of Weak Webhook Secret](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-vcx4-4qxg-mfp4) · Github advisories
2. Run the reusable agent prompt against the repo.
4. Notify user: "Restart Codex to pick up new skills."
- Add one new tactic based on Sign in.
- Rewrite one stale section with today’s source language.
- Turn the top signal into a reusable agent prompt.
- When encountering permission/DL failures, fall back to git sparse-checkout (HTTPS then SSH) and surface the exact error to the user.
- [OpenClaw: Telegram Webhook Missing Guess Rate Limiting Enables Brute-Force Guessing of Weak Webhook Secret](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-vcx4-4qxg-mfp4) · Github advisories
- Add one new tactic based on Sign in.
- Rewrite one stale section with today’s source language.
- Turn the top signal into a reusable agent prompt.
- [OpenClaw: Telegram Webhook Missing Guess Rate Limiting Enables Brute-Force Guessing of Weak Webhook Secret](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-vcx4-4qxg-mfp4) · Github advisories

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.summary: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

All of these scripts use network, so when running in the sandbox, request escalation when running them.
- Defaults to direct download for public GitHub repos.
- If download fails with auth/permission errors, falls back to git sparse checkout.
- Aborts if the destination skill directory already exists.
- Multiple `--path` values install multiple skills in one run, each named from the path basename unless `--name` is supplied.
- Curated listing is fetched from `https://github.com/openai/skills/tree/main/skills/.curated` via the GitHub API. If it is unavailable, explain the error and exit.
- Private GitHub repos can be accessed via existing git credentials or optional `GITHUB_TOKEN`/`GH_TOKEN` for download.
- Git fallback tries HTTPS first, then SSH.

- The skills at https://github.com/openai/skills/tree/main/skills/.system are preinstalled, so no need to help users install those. If they ask, just explain this. If they insist, you can download and overwrite.
The biggest delta is OpenClaw: Silent privilege escalation via gateway shared-auth reconnect. Fold the concrete changes into the operating notes, then discard the fluff.

items.title: Sign in
Search syntax tips
Sign up
Top 10 web hacking techniques of 2025
items.summary: Welcome to the Top 10 Web Hacking Techniques of 2025, the 19th edition of our annual community-powered effort to identify the most innovative must-read web security research published in the last year

items.title: Top 10 web hacking techniques of 2025: call for nominations
items.summary: Update: nominations are now closed, and voting is live! Cast your vote here Over the last year, security researchers have shared a huge amount of work with the community through blog posts, presentati

items.title: The Fragile Lock: Novel Bypasses For SAML Authentication
items.summary: TLDR This post shows how to achieve a full authentication bypass in the Ruby and PHP SAML ecosystem by exploiting several parser-level inconsistencies: including attribute pollution, namespace confusi

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.summary: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.summary: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.summary: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

diffLines.value: Helps install skills. By default these are from https://github.com/openai/skills/tree/main/skills/.curated, but users can also provide other locations. Experimental skills live in https://github.com/openai/skills/tree/main/skills/.experimental and can be installed the same way.
Use the helper scripts based on the task:

- List skills when the user asks what is available, or if the user uses this skill without specifying what to do. Default listing is `.curated`, but you can pass `--path skills/.experimental` when they ask about experimental skills.
- Install from the curated list when the user provides a skill name.
- Install from another repo when the user provides a GitHub repo/path (including private repos).
Install skills with the helper scripts.

When listing skills, output approximately as follows, depending on the context of the user's request. If they ask about experimental skills, list from `.experimental` instead of `.curated` and label the source accordingly:
Which ones would you like installed?
After installing a skill, tell the user: "Restart Codex to pick up new skills."

- Private GitHub repos can be accessed via existing git credentials or optional `GITHUB_TOKEN`/`GH_TOKEN` for download.
- Git fallback tries HTTPS first, then SSH.

- The skills at https://github.com/openai/skills/tree/main/skills/.system are preinstalled, so no need to help users install those. If they ask, just explain this. If they insist, you can download and overwrite.

items.title: The React Foundation: A New Home for React Hosted by the Linux Foundation
items.summary: The React Foundation has officially launched, hosted by the Linux Foundation.

items.title: Denial of Service and Source Code Exposure in React Server Components
items.summary: Security researchers have found and disclosed two additional vulnerabilities in React Server Components while attempting to exploit the patches in last week’s critical vulnerability. High vulnerability Denial of Service (CVE-2025-55184), and medium vulnerability Source Code Exposure (CVE-2025-55183)

items.title: Critical Security Vulnerability in React Server Components
items.summary: There is an unauthenticated remote code execution vulnerability in React Server Components. A fix has been published in versions 19.0.1, 19.1.2, and 19.2.1. We recommend upgrading immediately.
